subject: Mathematics

unit.unitOverview: Welcome to Unit 14! In this unit you will become confident with two of the most useful measurements in all of mathematics: perimeter and area. Perimeter is the distance all the way around the outside of a shape, and area is the amount of flat space inside it. These two ideas may sound similar, but they answer very different questions, and telling them apart is one of the key skills you will master here.
learningPath: Preview the goals and core ideas
Learn methods and study worked examples
Complete guided practice, activities and games
Apply, explain and complete the Unit Challenge

provenance.transformation: Structured from the Cambridge Primary Mathematics 0096 — Stage 4 workbook source documents for screen presentation.

outcomes: Explain the difference between area and perimeter in your own words.
Measure and calculate perimeter using linear units (mm, cm, m, km).
Measure and calculate area using square units (mm², cm², m², km²).
Estimate the area of a shape by counting whole and partial squares.
Use the formulas for the perimeter and area of rectangles and squares.
Choose the most sensible unit for measuring a given object.
Convert between different units of length and of area.
Find the area of a compound shape by splitting it into rectangles.
Solve real-world problems involving area and perimeter.

concepts.title: What Perimeter Really Means
concepts.explanation: Perimeter is the distance all the way around the outside edge of a flat shape. Imagine a tiny ant walking around the very edge of the shape until it returns to where it started. The total distance that ant walks is the perimeter. Because perimeter is a distance, it is a length, and we measure it in ordinary linear units such as millimetres (mm), centimetres (cm), metres (m) or kilometres (km). To find the perimeter of any straight-sided shape, you simply add up the lengths of all its sides. Nothing more clever…
concepts.example: Think of the real reasons you might need a perimeter. When Hassan fences the goat pen, the amount of fencing wire he must buy is exactly the perimeter of the pen. When Khadija sews a decorative border around the edge of…

concepts.title: What Area Really Means
concepts.explanation: Area is the amount of flat space inside a shape – how much surface it covers. Instead of walking around the edge like the ant, imagine covering the whole inside of the shape with small identical squares, leaving no gaps and no overlaps. The number of squares it takes to cover the shape is its area. Because area is measured in squares, we use square units: square millimetres (mm²), square centimetres (cm²), square metres (m²) or square kilometres (km²). The little ² after the unit is important. It is read as…
concepts.example: Think of the real reasons you might need an area. When Amina lays a mat to cover a floor, the size of mat she needs is the area of the floor. When Yusuf paints a wall, the amount of paint depends on the area of the…

concepts.title: Telling Area and Perimeter Apart
concepts.explanation: This is the idea that separates confident learners from confused ones, so let us make it crystal clear. Perimeter and area answer different questions about the same shape: Perimeter asks: how far is it around the edge? It is a length, measured in mm, cm, m or km.
concepts.example: Area asks: how much space is inside? It is a covering of squares, measured in mm², cm², m² or km².

concepts.title: Choosing the Right Units
concepts.explanation: Choosing a sensible unit is part of doing measurement well. We do not measure the distance between Mogadishu and Nairobi in millimetres, and we do not measure a postage stamp in kilometres. The unit should suit the size of the thing being measured. For perimeter and length, use linear units:
concepts.example: Millimetres (mm) – for very small objects, like a coin or a bead.

concepts.title: Perimeter and Area of a Rectangle by Formula
concepts.explanation: For rectangles and squares, we have neat formulas that let us find perimeter and area quickly without counting or adding every side one at a time. A formula is just a rule written with symbols that works every single time. A rectangle has four sides, and opposite sides are equal, so there are really only two different side lengths: the length and the width. There are two easy ways to find the perimeter.
concepts.example: Method 1 – add all four sides: Perimeter = length + width + length + width, which is the same as 2 × length + 2 × width. Method 2 – add length and width first, then double: Perimeter = 2 × (length + width). Both methods…

concepts.title: Finding a Missing Side
concepts.explanation: Sometimes a problem gives you the perimeter or area and one side, and asks you to work backwards to find the missing side. This is where understanding the formulas – rather than just memorising them – really pays off. Missing side from perimeter. Suppose a rectangle has a perimeter of 24 cm and a length of 7 cm. We know 2 × (length + width) = 24, so length + width = 12. Since the length is 7, the width must be 12 − 7 = 5 cm. A quick way to think about it: half the perimeter is always one length plus one width, so…
concepts.example: Missing side from area. Suppose a rectangle has an area of 24 cm² and a length of 6 cm. We know length × width = 24, so 6 × width = 24. We ask what times 6 makes 24, and the answer is 4, so the width is 4 cm. Finding a…

methods.title: How to find area and perimeter of a rectangle
methods.example: Find the perimeter of a rectangle that is 8 cm long and 5 cm wide.
steps: Read off the length and the width, with their units.
For perimeter, add length and width, then double.
For area, multiply length by width.
Write each answer with the correct unit – a plain unit for perimeter, a square unit for area.

methods.title: How to find a missing side
methods.example: Find the area of a rectangle that is 8 cm long and 5 cm wide.
steps: From perimeter: halve the perimeter, then subtract the side you know.
From area: divide the area by the side you know.
Check your answer by putting it back into the formula.

methods.title: How to find the area of a compound shape
methods.example: A square tile has sides of 9 cm. Find its perimeter and its area.
steps: Split the shape into rectangles with a straight line.
Find the length and width of each rectangle.
Find each area with length × width.
Add all the areas together.

methods.title: Finding a missing width from perimeter
methods.example: A rectangle has a perimeter of 30 m and a width of 5 m. What is its length?
steps: Solution: Half the perimeter is one length plus one width: 30 ÷ 2 = 15 m.
Take away the width: 15 − 5 = 10 m.
Correct.

methods.title: Finding a missing width from area
methods.example: A rectangle has an area of 35 m² and a width of 5 m. What is its length?
steps: Solution: Since Area = length × width, we have length × 5 = 35.
Divide: length = 35 ÷ 5 = 7 m.
Check: 7 × 5 = 35 m².
Correct.

methods.title: Estimating area by counting squares
methods.example: A spilled patch of water on squared paper covers 10 whole squares and 6 squares that are each more than half filled. Estimate its area.
steps: Solution: Count each more-than-half square as one: 10 + 6 = about 16 square units.
This is an estimate, because the part squares are judged by eye.
Check the estimating area by counting squares result carefully.

workedExamples.title: Perimeter of a rectangle
workedExamples.prompt: Find the perimeter of a rectangle that is 8 cm long and 5 cm wide.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Use Perimeter = 2 × (length + width) = 2 × (8 + 5) = 2 × 13 = 26 cm. Check by adding all four sides: 8 + 5 + 8 + 5 = 26 cm. The answer uses cm because perimeter is a length.

workedExamples.title: Area of a rectangle
workedExamples.prompt: Find the area of a rectangle that is 8 cm long and 5 cm wide.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Use Area = length × width = 8 × 5 = 40 cm². Picture 5 rows with 8 squares in each row: 5 eights make 40. The answer uses cm² because area is measured in squares.

workedExamples.title: Perimeter and area of a square
workedExamples.prompt: A square tile has sides of 9 cm. Find its perimeter and its area.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Perimeter = 4 × side = 4 × 9 = 36 cm. Area = side × side = 9 × 9 = 81 cm². Notice how the two answers have very different sizes and different units, even though they come from the same square.

workedExamples.title: Finding a missing width from perimeter
workedExamples.prompt: A rectangle has a perimeter of 30 m and a width of 5 m. What is its length?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Half the perimeter is one length plus one width: 30 ÷ 2 = 15 m. Take away the width: 15 − 5 = 10 m. Check: 2 × (10 + 5) = 2 × 15 = 30 m. Correct.

workedExamples.title: Finding a missing width from area
workedExamples.prompt: A rectangle has an area of 35 m² and a width of 5 m. What is its length?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Since Area = length × width, we have length × 5 = 35. Divide: length = 35 ÷ 5 = 7 m. Check: 7 × 5 = 35 m². Correct.

workedExamples.title: Estimating area by counting squares
workedExamples.prompt: A spilled patch of water on squared paper covers 10 whole squares and 6 squares that are each more than half filled. Estimate its area.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Count each more-than-half square as one: 10 + 6 = about 16 square units. This is an estimate, because the part squares are judged by eye.

workedExamples.title: Area of a compound shape
workedExamples.prompt: An L-shaped floor splits into a 6 m × 4 m rectangle and a 3 m × 2 m rectangle. Find the total area.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Rectangle 1: 6 × 4 = 24 m². Rectangle 2: 3 × 2 = 6 m². Add them: 24 + 6 = 30 m². That is how much matting would cover the whole floor.

workedExamples.title: A converting problem
workedExamples.prompt: A garden fence is 7 m long. How many centimetres is that? And if a room has an area of 4 m², how many cm² is that?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: For length, multiply by 100: 7 m = 7 × 100 = 700 cm. For area, multiply by 100 × 100 = 10 000: 4 m² = 4 × 10 000 = 40 000 cm². Length uses 100; area uses 100 squared, which is 10 000.

workedExamples.title: Practice 9
workedExamples.prompt: A rectangle has an area of 24 cm² and a length of 6 cm. Find its width.

workedExamples.title: Practice 10
workedExamples.prompt: Convert: a) 5 m to cm b) 250 cm to m c) 3 km to m.

workedExamples.title: Practice 11
workedExamples.prompt: Convert: a) 7 m² to cm² b) 3000 mm² to cm².

workedExamples.title: Practice 12
workedExamples.prompt: Five rectangles all have a perimeter of 24 units: 10 × 2, 9 × 3, 8 × 4, 7 × 5 and 6 × 6. Find the area of each. Which has the largest area, and what do you notice?
workedExamples.solution: 1) Areas: 10 × 2 = 20, 9 × 3 = 27, 8 × 4 = 32, 7 × 5 = 35, 6 × 6 = 36 square units. The 6 × 6 square has the largest area – for a fixed perimeter, the square holds the most space.

activities.title: Measure Your Room (Household Objects)
activities.materials: a tape measure or a metre ruler, paper, a pencil.
steps: Choose a rectangular room, mat or table top.
Measure the length and the width, and write them down with their units.
Work out the perimeter using 2 × (length + width).
Work out the area using length × width, and write the answer with a square unit.
Measure two more objects and record all your results in a neat table.

activities.title: Squared-Paper Rectangles
activities.materials: squared paper, a pencil, a ruler.
steps: Draw a rectangle 5 squares long and 3 squares wide.
Count the squares inside to find the area, then check with 5 × 3.
Count around the edge to find the perimeter, then check with 2 × (5 + 3).
Draw three more rectangles of different sizes and do the same.
Colour the perimeter of each rectangle in one colour and shade the area in another.

activities.title: The Same-Area Challenge (Math Game)
activities.materials: squared paper and a pencil, one or two players.
steps: Agree on a target area, for example 24 squares.
Each player draws as many different rectangles with that exact area as they can (for example 1 × 24, 2 × 12, 3 × 8, 4 × 6).
Work out the perimeter of each rectangle you drew.
The player who found the rectangle with the smallest perimeter wins the round.
Talk about why the most square-like rectangle wins every time.

activities.title: Estimate the Leaf (Investigation)
activities.materials: squared paper, a leaf or a flat stone, a pencil.
steps: Place the leaf on the squared paper and draw around its edge.
Estimate the area first – make a sensible guess in squares.
Count the whole squares inside your outline.
Count the part squares that are more than half covered, and add them on.
Compare your careful count with your first estimate. How close were you?

activities.title: Design a Garden (Family Task)
activities.materials: squared paper, coloured pencils, your family's ideas.
steps: Let each square stand for 1 metre by 1 metre of ground.
Design a rectangular or L-shaped garden on the paper.
Work out the perimeter, so you know how much fencing to buy.
Work out the area, so you know how much space there is for planting.
Split any L-shape into two rectangles to find its area, then add them.

activities.title: Compound-Shape Floor Plan
activities.materials: squared paper, a ruler, a pencil.
steps: Draw an L-shaped or T-shaped room on the squared paper.
Cut the shape into two rectangles with a single straight line.
Find the area of each rectangle and add them for the total area.
Trace right around the outside to find the perimeter, counting every edge.
Swap plans with a partner and check each other's totals.

rules.title: Key idea 1
rules.text: A rule written with symbols that works every time

rules.title: Key idea 2
rules.text: For rectangles and squares, we have neat formulas that let us find perimeter and area quickly without counting or adding every side one at a time. A formula is just a rule written with symbols that works every single time.

rules.title: Key idea 3
rules.text: The method has a clever rule for the awkward squares along the edge that are only partly covered:

rules.title: Key idea 4
rules.text: 🤖 Ask Your AI Tutor Ask your AI tutor to describe a shape drawn on a grid and let you practise counting whole and part squares. Say: Explain again the rule for counting squares that are only partly covered.

terms: The distance all the way around a shape
The amount of flat space inside a shape
A length unit: mm, cm, m, km
An area unit: mm², cm², m², km²
Four sides, four right angles, opposite sides equal
A rectangle with all four sides equal
A shape made from two or more rectangles
A careful guess before measuring exactly
commonMistakes: Ask: around the edge, or inside the shape?
Always add the ²: 28 cm², not 28 cm
Area is length × width, not length + width

reasoningPrompts.prompt: A square has an area of 49 cm². What is the length of each side, and what is its perimeter?
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: 7 × 7 = 49, so each side is 7 cm; perimeter 4 × 7 = 28 cm.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: An L-shape splits into a 4 m × 3 m rectangle and a 2 m × 2 m rectangle. Find its total area.
A rectangular field is 25 m by 15 m. A rectangular flower bed 5 m by 3 m sits inside it. Find the area of the field that is not flower bed.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Field 25 × 15 = 375 m²; flower bed 5 × 3 = 15 m²; not flower bed = 375 − 15 = 360 m².

selfAssessment: I can explain the difference between area and perimeter in your own words.
I can measure and calculate perimeter using linear units (mm, cm, m, km).
I can measure and calculate area using square units (mm², cm², m², km²).
I can estimate the area of a shape by counting whole and partial squares.
I can use the formulas for the perimeter and area of rectangles and squares.
I can choose the most sensible unit for measuring a given object.
I can convert between different units of length and of area.
I can find the area of a compound shape by splitting it into rectangles.
